messages: Update: I just got a message from the recruiter with a big change to the format.

Instead of the in-person process we've been planning, they now want a single recorded submission where I answer behavioral prompts and demonstrate a short product walkthrough plus a light coding example.
The timeline changed too—the window to produce the recording is much shorter than I expected, so I'll need to prepare and record almost immediately.
That eliminates the possibility of an in-person whiteboard and means I must convey technical ability through screen capture only.
This really undercuts the two-week in-person rehearsal plan I was building, so a lot of my prep needs to be rethought.
I have a laptop with a webcam and screen-recording software and access to a sandbox repo, but no external camera or studio setup.
I can do basic edits—cuts, captions, splicing takes—but nothing elaborate, and the recruiters said the submission should be self-contained.
I need to be concise and credible in a single take or two, showing product thinking, communication, and some technical fluency.
Given all that, please help me reprioritize: what should I focus on immediately to make the most persuasive short submission?
Also, how should I repurpose the stories and design practice I already planned to fit this new format?
Given this pivot to a single compact recording made on my laptop, can you outline a minute-by-minute script for the video: which stories to open with, how to transition to the demo, and how to close?
How can I compress STAR answers even more so they hit the key beats quickly without sounding rushed or superficial?
For the screen-captured demo and simple code sample, what's the most reliable way to set up and present them so they appear polished and honest with basic tools?
What should I rehearse to perfection and what can I safely improvise during recording to keep the delivery natural under time pressure?
Any straightforward framing, lighting, and quick editing advice to make the result feel professional when I only have a laptop and one evening to produce it?
Lastly, what single sentence or moment should I use at the end to leave the strongest, most memorable impression in such a compact format?
